deconstruction_targets: The idea that economic relations have a natural or inevitable sequence
Historicist notions of wealth creation for the state
Class structures as fixed and essential (capital, wage labor, landed property)
State debt and public credit as inherent to bourgeois society
Colonies and emigration as structural features
Uneven development of material production vs. cultural/artistic development
Legal relations (e.g., Roman private law) shaping economic development
summary: The text deconstructs the naturalization of capitalist social relations by exposing their historical contingency, class specificity, and ideological masking. It critiques abstract determinism, historicist teleology, and moral justifications for wealth accumulation in modern states. While rhetorically charged with moral condemnation of bourgeois structures and intellectual traditions (e.g., Proudhon, 17th–18th century economists), it offers no concrete constructive alternatives—only a dismantling of existing frameworks.
